Sample Digest · Monday 27 April 2026

A recent Geriatric Medicine digest,
as sent to subscribers

This is the real Monday digest from Monday 27 April 2026. Showing the first 8 of 28 articles from that week — every new paper from the top journals in Geriatric Medicine.

28articles that week
5journals covered
£0for your first 4 weeks
Note: Summaries are AI-generated from publicly available PubMed abstracts. Always verify against the source article before clinical use.
Age and ageing

Heat-related deaths in social care in England: can the Care Quality Commission ratings system be used to identify care homes that would benefit most from heat adaptation measures?

Hajat S, Hussein S, Li K et al. · 2026 Apr 04
Study Type: Time-series regression analysis using national data
Key Question: What is the risk of heat-related mortality in English care homes, and can CQC ratings identify facilities needing priority heat adaptation measures?
Key Findings:
  • During July 2022 heatwave, nursing home deaths increased by 34.1% (95% CI 21.1-48.2%) and residential care deaths by 13.0% (0.1-27.0%)
  • On 25°C days versus 16°C days (2022-24), death risk was 2.09 times higher in nursing homes (95% CI 1.68-2.60) and 1.56 times higher in residential care (1.24-1.96)
  • Poorer CQC ratings showed gradient of increasing heat mortality risk, with highest risks in West Midlands and London
Clinical Relevance: This demonstrates substantial heat vulnerability among care home residents across England, providing evidence for urgent implementation of heat adaptation strategies in social care settings serving our frailest older adults.
Limitations: Study period limited to recent years may not capture longer-term temperature-mortality relationships or account for evolving care practices.
PubMed →DOI →
Age and ageing

Dual-threshold plasma Aβ oligomerization for postoperative delirium risk stratification

Yang Y, Jung KJ, Kwak YT · 2026 Apr 04
Study Type: Prospective cohort study
Key Question: Can preoperative plasma amyloid-β oligomer levels predict postoperative delirium risk in older surgical patients?
Key Findings:
  • Among 101 patients ≥65 years having orthopaedic surgery, 44% developed postoperative delirium
  • Preoperative plasma MDS-OAβ discriminated delirium with AUC 0.855 (95% CI 0.777-0.919)
  • Dual-threshold approach (0.60 and 0.85 ng/ml) identified low-risk and high-risk groups with high negative and positive predictive values respectively
Clinical Relevance: This biomarker could enable preoperative risk stratification for delirium in NHS elective surgery, potentially allowing targeted prevention strategies and enhanced monitoring for high-risk patients.
Limitations: Single-centre study limited to orthopaedic patients may not generalise to other surgical populations or healthcare settings.
PubMed →DOI →
Age and ageing

Effects of VIVIFRAIL intervention on multidimensional frailty in older nursing home residents: a randomised-controlled feasibility study

Pradana AA, Hidayat AT, Yanti L et al. · 2026 Apr 04
Study Type: Randomised controlled feasibility trial
Key Question: Is the VIVIFRAIL exercise programme feasible and effective for improving frailty in nursing home residents?
Key Findings:
  • Excellent feasibility metrics: 100% recruitment/adherence rates, 7.5% attrition, 86-87% satisfaction among 80 participants
  • Significant improvements in frailty status (P=0.002), physical performance (P=0.001), activities of daily living (P<0.001), and quality of life (P<0.001)
  • Benefits sustained across all measured time points with significant between-group differences (P<0.001)
Clinical Relevance: Provides evidence that structured exercise interventions can be successfully implemented in UK care homes to address the high prevalence of frailty, potentially informing commissioning and care planning decisions.
Limitations: Short study duration (3 months) limits understanding of long-term sustainability and effectiveness.
PubMed →DOI →
Age and ageing

Prevalence and prognostic relevance of perioperative myocardial injury/infarction after major noncardiac surgery in older patients

Ergin E, Durak K, Glarner N et al. · 2026 Apr 04
Study Type: Prospective multicentre cohort study
Key Question: What is the prevalence and prognostic impact of perioperative myocardial injury/infarction in older patients undergoing major noncardiac surgery?
Key Findings:
  • PMI occurred in 19.2% of older patients (≥70 years with ≥3 comorbidities, or ≥80 years), significantly higher than younger patients
  • One-year mortality was 26.2% with PMI versus 13.2% without PMI; MACE rates were 30% versus 13% respectively
  • Highest risk period was postoperative day 1 (mortality HR 10.5, 95% CI 4.5-24.5), declining but persisting through one year
Clinical Relevance: This large study demonstrates that nearly one in five older surgical patients develop PMI with doubled mortality risk, informing perioperative risk stratification and monitoring strategies in UK geriatric surgical populations.
Limitations: The study focused on patients already at increased cardiovascular risk, potentially limiting generalisability to all older surgical patients.
PubMed →DOI →
Age and ageing

Coronary arterial age for mortality prediction and risk reclassification in SCORE COVID-19 registry: When vascular age runs faster or slower than chronological age

Cereda A, Toselli M, Palmisano A et al. · 2026 Apr 04
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: Does coronary arterial age derived from calcium scoring improve mortality prediction compared to chronological age in hospitalized COVID-19 patients?
Key Findings:
  • Coronary arterial age and chronological age showed similar discrimination for 30-day mortality (AUC 0.76 vs 0.74, P=0.466)
  • Coronary arterial age improved specificity by correctly identifying survivors who would otherwise be classified as high-risk
  • Among high-risk patients (4C score), the difference between vascular and chronological age (ΔAge) significantly refined mortality risk stratification
Clinical Relevance: This supports using coronary calcium scoring from routine chest CTs to refine prognosis in older adults with COVID-19, particularly for identifying lower-risk patients among those with high clinical risk scores.
Limitations: Retrospective design limits causal inference and the findings may not generalize beyond COVID-19 hospitalized patients.
PubMed →DOI →
Age and ageing

How does diagnostic subtype affect the quality of primary care for people with dementia? A retrospective cohort study in 1490 English general practices

Morris C, Mok PLH, Robinson DL et al. · 2026 Apr 04
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: Does dementia diagnostic subtype affect the quality of primary care received by people with dementia in English general practice?
Key Findings:
  • Among 571,663 people with dementia, those with mixed dementia subtypes were more likely to receive care plans (HR 1.29-1.37) compared to Alzheimer's disease, while all other subtypes were less likely
  • All non-Alzheimer's subtypes had higher rates of potentially inappropriate prescribing across four indicators (anticholinergics, z-drugs, benzodiazepines, antipsychotics)
  • 72.1% received care plans and 79.4% received medication reviews within 24 months of diagnosis
Clinical Relevance: This demonstrates systematic inequities in dementia care quality across diagnostic subtypes in UK primary care, which could inform targeted quality improvement initiatives and care pathway redesign.
Limitations: Retrospective design using coded data may not capture all clinical decision-making rationale or unmeasured confounding factors affecting prescribing appropriateness.
PubMed →DOI →
Age and ageing

Healthy ageing in urban settings: neighbourhood environments modify the association between intrinsic capacity and social participation among older adults in Brazil

Guarita MLC, Bertola L, Covinsky KE et al. · 2026 Apr 04
Study Type: Cross-sectional study
Key Question: How do neighbourhood environments modify the relationship between intrinsic capacity and social participation in older Brazilian adults?
Key Findings:
  • Higher intrinsic capacity was associated with increased social participation (adjusted prevalence ratio 1.31 per 1-SD increase, 95% CI 1.22-1.40)
  • Supportive neighbourhoods significantly modified this relationship, particularly benefiting those with low intrinsic capacity
  • Among low intrinsic capacity adults, predicted probability of high social participation ranged from 12% in least supportive to 30% in most supportive neighbourhoods
Clinical Relevance: This demonstrates that environmental interventions could compensate for declining intrinsic capacity in maintaining social engagement—relevant for UK geriatricians considering community-based approaches to healthy ageing and social prescribing initiatives.
Limitations: Cross-sectional design prevents determination of causal relationships between neighbourhood factors and social participation outcomes.
PubMed →DOI →
Age and ageing

Effects of Tai Chi combined with elastic band training on muscle and executive inhibitory function in older adults with sarcopenia: a randomized controlled trial based on fNIRS

Zhan S, Wu S, Ma Q et al. · 2026 Apr 04
Study Type: Randomized controlled trial
Key Question: Does combining Tai Chi with elastic band training provide superior benefits compared to elastic band training alone for muscle function and executive cognitive performance in older adults with sarcopenia?
Key Findings:
  • Both elastic band training alone and combined Tai Chi/elastic band training improved grip strength, muscle mass, physical performance, and cognitive reaction times after 12 weeks, while health education showed no benefits
  • The combined intervention group demonstrated significantly greater improvements in physical performance, executive inhibitory function, and prefrontal cortex activation compared to elastic band training alone
  • Combined training uniquely improved cognitive task accuracy, suggesting additional cognitive benefits beyond resistance training alone
Clinical Relevance: This provides evidence-based support for incorporating Tai Chi alongside resistance training in sarcopenia management programmes, potentially offering both physical and cognitive benefits relevant to falls prevention and functional independence in UK older adult services.
Limitations: Small sample size (49 participants) limits generalisability and statistical power for detecting smaller effect differences between interventions.
PubMed →DOI →
